coredata.dc:title: Effect of anode position on the incorporation of nano/microparticles during the PEO coating on AZ31B
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Esmaeili, Mehdi
Asgari, Masoud
Daneshmand, Hamid
Karimi, Meysam
Rouhaghdam, Alireza Sabour
coredata.dc:description: In this study, the influence of the sample position (anode) on the incorporation of nano/micro alumina particles on the AZ31B magnesium alloy substrate was investigated by plasma electrolytic oxidation (PEO) coating. For characterized this effect, Scanning electron microscopy (SEM), potentiodynamic polarization (PDP), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and pin-on-disk test were used to the evolution of the microstructure, corrosion resistance, and the wear behavior of the films. Based on the obtained results, the effect of the anode position from vertical to horizontal is dependent on particle size and it can be influenced only on the incorporation of nanoparticles. The experimental corrosion tests showed that maximum corrosion resistance occurred in the horizontal position of the anode electrode in the presence of alumina nanoparticles in the optimum condition (current density was reduced up to 1.8 nA/cm2 and the wear rate has decreased to 27 µg/N.m).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Plasma electrolytic oxidation
Magnesium
Alumina
Corrosion
Wear
Anode position

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The TEM image of alumina NPs and SEM image alumina MPs.
Schematic of the position of the anode relative to the cathode (a) horizontal (b) vertical.
Graph of weight variations samples coated by the PEO method in horizontally and vertically positions of the anode.
SEM images of variations samples coated by the PEO method in horizontally and vertically positions of the anode.
The cross-section of variations samples coated by the PEO method in horizontally and vertically positions of the anode.
The thickness of samples coated by PEO in horizontally and vertically positions of the anode.
The porosity percentage of the samples coated by PEO in horizontally and vertically positions of the anode.
Roughness average (Ra) of the samples coated by PEO in horizontally and vertically positions of the anode.
XRD pattern of the sample is coated in a horizontal position without additives.
EDS map of the sample with 30 g/L aluminas with the current density of 100 mA/cm2.
Schematic of a different anode position of coating treatment in the electrolyte containing NPs.
Potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) curves of the samples coated by the PEO method in horizontally and vertically positions of the anode.
Curves of electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of (a) N-H (b) M-H, M-V, N-V, and PEO samples coated by the PEO method in horizontally and vertically positions of the anode.
Curves of (a) Bode-bode, and (b) Bode-phase of samples coated by the PEO method in horizontally and vertically positions of the anode.
The equivalent circuit is used for fitting the impedance data.
Wear coefficient curves of the samples coated by the PEO method in horizontally and vertically positions of the anode.
Wear rate of the samples coated by the PEO method in horizontally and vertically positions of the anode.
SEM images of worn surfaces of PEO coatings fabricated at (a) PEO, (b) M-H, (c) M-V, (d) N-H and (e) N-V condition.
Chemical composition of the AZ31B magnesium alloy substrate.
Conditions for the coating substrates.
The appellation of the coated samples.
Results extracted from potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) curves of the samples coated by PEO method in horizontally and vertically positions of the anode.
Results extracted from electrochemical impedance curves of the samples coated by the PEO method in horizontally and vertically positions of the anode.
Wear properties data for coating fabricated at different condition.

head.ce:title: Effect of anode position on the incorporation of nano/microparticles during the PEO coating on AZ31B

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: dDepartment of Materials Engineering, Shahreza Branch, Islamic Azad University, P.O. Box: 86145-311, Shahreza, Isfahan, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, the influence of the sample position (anode) on the incorporation of nano/micro alumina particles on the AZ31B magnesium alloy substrate was investigated by plasma electrolytic oxidation (PEO) coating. For characterized this effect, Scanning electron microscopy (SEM), potentiodynamic polarization (PDP), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), and pin-on-disk test were used to the evolution of the microstructure, corrosion resistance, and the wear behavior of the films. Based on the obtained results, the effect of the anode position from vertical to horizontal is dependent on particle size and it can be influenced only on the incorporation of nanoparticles. The experimental corrosion tests showed that maximum corrosion resistance occurred in the horizontal position of the anode electrode in the presence of alumina nanoparticles in the optimum condition (current density was reduced up to 1.8 nA/cm2 and the wear rate has decreased to 27 µg/N.m).

ce:section.ce:para: Magnesium alloys are extremely attractive for industrial. It can be used in automobile, aerospace, electronics, computers, cell phones, transportation, biomedical, and energy sectors due to excellent properties such as high strength to weight ratio, low density, good damping performance, biocompatibility, recyclability, large hydrogen storage capacity, and high theoretical specific capacity for the battery [1–3]. On the other hand, the high chemical activity of magnesium alloys causes the resistance of corrosion to be decreased. This subject causes limitations for practical applications of these materials. Therefore, it is inevitable to utilize specific methods for improving the surface of magnesium alloys [4–7].

To overcome this problem, the anti-corrosion coatings methods including electrodeposition, organic films, physical vapor deposition (PVD), and plasma electrolyte oxidations (PEO) are widely developed and applied in this field [4,8–10]. Among these techniques, PEO coating is a simple and inexpensive method. It can enhance the protection ability of magnesium alloys from high corrosion phenomena [11–13]. Despite the mentioned benefits factor for the PEO method, this way may have other disadvantages such as porous morphology and permeability [14–16]. Happening these events can because destruction in corrosion applications. Thus, it is needed to fill the film porosity with some particles [2,17,18]. In the past decade, researchers use ceramic particles and polymer post-treatment for improving properties of PEO coatings [11–13]. Among them, ceramic particles have received more attention because they increase corrosion and wear properties.

Recently, nanostructured films are the most widely used materials in the protection system against corrosion because they have unique chemical-physical properties [19]. Excellent mechanical and chemical properties can be caused by high hardness, high toughness, low friction, high adhesion strength to the substrate, and good chemical and thermal stability for these materials (nanostructured films and coatings) [20,21]. There is much research in this field [22]. For example, Sharifi et al. [23–25] investigated the mechanical properties of a nanocomposite coating reinforced with alumina nanoparticles created by the PEO method on titanium substrate. They reported that alumina additive causes these nanoparticles (NPs) to fill the volcanic-like valleys created by the process, reducing the difference between the high points and the deeper parts of the coating of surface roughness. Therefore, the amount of roughness will be decreases and this reduction in roughness will be intensified by increasing the concentration of NPs. Regarding the hardness of the coating, the obtained results from their work were shown when the concentration of NPs increase in the electrolyte, a greater increase happened in the hardness. The wear resistance of these coatings, which was examined by the wear rate. According to the obtained results for this test, the wear resistance of the coatings was enhanced with the increasing concentration of NPs. Based on the obtained results, it is found numerous parameters can be influenced in the process of PEO such as the type of applied current density, anode voltage range, electrolyte composition, and concentration [26–28].

Many researchers reported an improvement effect in corrosion and wear resistance by adding alumina NPs in their work [29]. Wang et al. [30] evaluated the effect of alumina NPs on corrosion resistance and hardness of PEO coating. They reported that the dispersion of particles in the solution had a significant effect on the quality of the coating. Aliofkhazraei et al. [31,32] reported that the NPs incorporate inside the coating during the PEO process in a short direction. Lee and co-workers [33] in their work reported that electrophoretic force and mechanical turbulence in molten oxide are the two important factors in NPs incorporation. According to the obtained results [34], the size and melting point for particles are important in the incorporation process and NPs incorporation occurs during the micro spark.

Based on the provided mechanism by these researchers, it can be concluded that the size of coating pores and affected forces on NPs movement can be strongly influenced by the NPs incorporation. Additionally, the chemical composition of electrolytes is a noteworthy and important factor in the structure and properties of nanocomposite films [35]. Changing the chemical composition of the electrolyte can be a suitable factor in the production of PEO films with excellent properties. Asgari et al. [18] investigated the effect of urea on the PEO process and they found that the addition of urea not only increased the nitrogen content in the films but also reduced its thickness and increased precipitants in the electrolyte. According to the obtained results, it may be concluded that various additives change the surface of particles so that NPs may be better incorporated [36–38].

In our previous investigations, it is studied the effect of various parameters such as stirring rate, concentration, and size of alumina particles, and their incorporation on magnesium surface using PEO coating [20,30]. Due to obtained results, the highest amount of NPs incorporation obtain at the velocity of 100 rpm and decreases at a high stirring rate. The reason for the happening of this phenomenon is in the high turbulence of electrolytes, in which the NPs were washed from the surface of the anode. Also, the effect of NPs concentration in the electrolyte on the alumina incorporation was studied. Results indicated that by increasing the concentration of NPs in the electrolyte, the incorporation of alumina increases up to 30 g/L; and then reaches the saturated level. Changing the weight indicated that the additives did not improve the incorporation of microparticles (MPs), whereas the incorporation of NPs was enhanced. The research showed that urea is the most efficient additive which was used.

Since the research about the various PEO parameters has provided valuable results (especially research in the effect of these parameters on the coverage of the films), it is necessary to further investigate to clarify the role of all parameters and their correlation with each other. With the consideration of the previous studies by researchers, the effect of the anode position has not been studied on the properties of PEO coatings yet. Fattah-alhosseini et al. [19] studied the effect of the substrate position on the nanocomposite coating by plating with diamond particles. They noted that the position of the sample has a significant effect on particle incorporation. Due to the similarity in the process of coating in plating and PEO, it can be predicted that the mentioned parameter (the anode position) has a significant effect on the incorporation of particles (nano or micro size).

In the present work, we focus on the effect of anode position in the PEO coating of alumina particles on AZ31B magnesium alloy. Therefore, to study the exact effect of the anode position in particle incorporation, at the same time with the mentioned parameter as well as the particle size were investigated.

α-Alumina from Merck was used. AZ31B magnesium alloy samples were used as substrate. It was cut in the dimensions of 4 × 2.5 × 0.2 cm3, sanded using sandpaper from 400# to 1500#, and then degreased in an acetone solution. Direct current and stainless steel 304 (as the cathode) were used for PEO coating. The chemical composition of the AZ31B magnesium alloy substrate was shown in Table 1 .

The coating electrolyte was made with the 5 g/L NaPO4, 10 g/L KOH, and 30 g/L alumina particles with sizes of between 50 nm and 300 nm (Fig. 1 ). For obtained homogeneous dispersion of particles, firstly, it was stirred in double-distilled water for 24 h. Next, the solution was homogenized for about 30 min by an ultrasonic probe before the coating process. The specifications of the coating conditions are shown in Table 2 . The anode was placed horizontally (Fig. 2 a) and vertically (Fig. 2b) in the electrolyte. To improve the homogenization of the particles, reduce the particle size, and increase the percentage of nanoparticle incorporation, 20 g/L urea was added to the electrolyte. The details for the investigation of the different conditions for the coated substrates are shown in Table 3 .

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (XL30 Philips model) with energy-dispersive, X-ray spectroscopy, and EDS (Noran 6) were used to investigate the porosity and morphology of the coating and the images were analyzed by ImageJ software. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern is obtained on a Philips; X'Pert MPD. X-ray diffractometer with Co Kαradiation (λ: 1.78897 Å) at a scanning rate of 0.02°/s was used to determine the crystal phase of the obtained composite. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) of NPs was taken by a JEOL -2010 TEM. The surface roughness of samples was obtained using a Portable roughness tester (TIME TR200). In addition, the porosity of the samples was obtained using image processing with image j software.

The corrosion behavior of the samples was investigated by the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and the potentiodynamic polarization (PDP) measurements in a 3.5 wt% sodium chloride solution. The electrochemical experiments were performed by the EG&G A273 device. A conventional three-electrode cell was used with the main sample as a working electrode, an Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode, and platinum as the counter electrode. The EIS measurements were carried out according to the ASTM G106 standard, at a frequency range between 65.5 kHz and 0.01 Hz at the open circuit potential (OCP) with a perturbation amplitude of 10 mV. The PDP measurements were carried out according to ASTM G5 standard, from a point with the potential 0.2 V, more negative than the OCP up to 0.8 V higher, where the voltage scan rate and perturbation were 1 mV/s.

The wear properties were investigated by a pin-on-disc wear test, applied load of 45 N, and speed of 100 rpm. The distance traveled was 200 m for all samples. Each test was repeated three times to ensure the reproducibility of the experiments. Also, the type of friction pair was polybon.

Various parameters are influenced by the incorporation of particles in the process of the PEO coating. One of these parameters is the position of the anode or sample into the electrolyte. Alumina is used as a particle in this work. These particles have a high melting point and low reactivity in working conditions and are not ionized into the electrolyte during coating.

In our previous research, the effect of mechanical force from electrolyte rotation was evaluated. According to the obtained results, when the electrolyte velocity increased, the particle incorporation decreased in the coating. Happening the agglomeration reduces the quality of the coating. The best speed was applied to all coated samples was obtained at 100 rpm [20], so this speed is used in this study. Force of weight is one of the important parameters during the process. The force of particle weight is not a transient effect in NPs, but this force can be effective by increasing the particle size. Particles must be transferred to the interface between the electrolyte and the sample (working electrode) during the PEO process. They must compensate for the decrease in concentration due to the incorporation of particles into the coating [28,39,40]. So, when the particles are dispersed into the coating, the particles into the electrolyte interact with the hydroxide ions, and the negative charge was formed on the surface of them. When the electric forces were applied to the coating, the particles moved and joined to the surface of the anode. According to these discussions, it can be concluded that the force of gravity can be a transient effect on the transfer of particles to the coating and their incorporation on the substrate [29,23]. Aliofkhazraei et al. [17,41] have considered other factors such as agglomeration of particles in the pores, current density, and eruption of molten oxide from the substrate (as the main factors for the entrance of the particles into the coating).

Fig. 3 shows the effect of the anode position (horizontal and vertical positions) with the incorporation of particles of different sizes in the solution. As shown in Fig. 3 the weight of the films (due to the incorporation of particles in the coating) is changed. Base on the obtained results, more changed in the weight indicate more incorporation particles in the coating. When the position of the anode was changed from vertical to horizontal, increases the change in the percentage of weight was increased. It should be that note in the horizontal position the incorporation percentage is high than the vertical position. The changing percentage of weight in the horizontal position was low in the presence of MPs.

Previous research was shown that urea is one of the best additives for NPs as well as for MPs and in presence of urea, the maximum incorporation of particles happened [12]. In this study, urea was added due to the prevention of agglomeration and homogeneously incorporation of particles. Adding urea to the solution did not change the incorporation percentage. Due to the saturation of the coating and the lack of preferred sites for the coating, it probably could not significantly increase the incorporation. Although a slight increase in the weight in the presence of the additive was observed. This phenomenon allows the particles to be better placed near the interface between the electrolyte and the sample. The percentage of MPs incorporation in the coating is low. So, the coating is not able to incorporate this size of particles. Overall, in PEO coatings, the places of particle incorporation in the coating are the stressful places of the films, such as micropores or cracks [32]. These particles fill the structural defects of the coating and cause the filling porosity of the films such as micro-cavities or cracks [26]. As these preferred sites are filled with particles, the coating placement factor decreases and the particle incorporation reaches a saturation level that occurs in the presence of NPs; and the addition of the urea did not cause significant changes in the nanoparticles.

The results of the EDS are shown in Fig. 3. According to the observation, EDS results are consistent with the weight change results. As shown in Fig. 3, the diagram for the aluminum element was changed with the anode position. Increasing the percentage of aluminum can be attributed to the presence of ceramic particles because aluminum was present only in the NPs and MPs. So it can be concluded when the position anode was changed from vertical to horizontal the incorporation of NPs was increased. This event was observed in both weight change and increasing the concentration of aluminum. However, changing the position of the anode could not cause a significant change in the incorporation of MPs. On the other hand, adding the urea did not change the weight and significantly increase the amount of aluminum in the coatings. As mentioned, this could be due to the saturation of coverage and the lack of preferred locations to attract NPs as well as agents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology, composition, and porosity of the coating
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 shows the SEM images of the PEO films in the presence of urea and particles of different sizes and various positions of the anode. The presence of different particles in the electrolyte and their incorporation in the coating has a great effect on the surface morphology, growth of the coating, and the amount of porosity. Pancake-shaped structures with different pore sizes can be seen in the structure of all samples. In NPs samples, the structure changed slightly and the cavities were filled with NPs. The presence of urea additive into the electrolyte did not change the overall structure of the surface. The researchers believe that the pancake structure formed during the PEO coating process and the presence of various holes in the coating surface are the results of drainage channels in the mechanism of this coating method. High-energy discharges or micro-sparks on the surface can be caused by pressures as well as very high temperatures. It will melt the material in the discharge urea. When these materials were released, the molten material collides with the electrolyte at a much lower temperature than molten oxide and freezes rapidly at room temperature. As a result, a pancake-shaped structure is created during this process [33,34].

In the center of each pancake, there are micro-cavities. So evacuation occurs. As can be seen from the SEM images, the change in the position of the anode has been effective in incorporation the NPs. Changing from the vertical to the horizontal position, the cavities are filled. These results are consistent with the obtained results from the incorporation percentage (Fig. 3). The existence of a structure without the presence of particles is almost obvious in the images of MPs. When the position of the anode was changed from the horizontal to the vertical, the presence of small amounts of particles was seen. The obtained images for samples show that the number of particles has slightly increased in the presence of urea. As can be seen from the SEM images, urea does not affect the structure of the particle. It probably has been an effect on particle dispersion. It can be believed that particles penetrate cavities and fill the cavities. When these procedures occurred, other properties such as wear and corrosion will be fill. Arrabal et al. [42,43] showed that increasing the concentration has reduced the surface porosity, which is seen in these results.

Due to the conversion of PEO coatings, these coatings have good adhesion to the substrate. Fig. 5 shows the cross-section of the coated specimens in horizontally and vertically positions of the anode. It is clear that all samples have suitable adhesion on the substrate and there are no defects in the interface of substrate-film [38,44]. Using the urea additive leads to the stability of the particle in the electrolyte. Therefore, the incorporation of NPs will be improved during the process and the voids were blocked in the coating. As a result, the thickness of the coating formed in the presence of NPs is less than the MPs and the quality of films will be better.

Fig. 6 shows the thickness variations of the coated samples. The approximation thickness of the coating is between 5.5 and 8.5 µm at different conditions. It is observed that by changing the position of the electrode from vertical to horizontal, the thickness of the coating was reduced in the presence of NPs into the electrolyte. However, the thickness does not change in the presence of MPs. Also, using the additives does not affect the thickness remarkably. Decreasing the thickness will be happening in the presence of NPs at the interface of electrolyte and substrate. In this state, the coating rapidly fills and prevents the melt to exit. Therefore the process is interrupted [22]. According to the weight gain and decrease of the coating thickness, it can be argued that the porosity of the coating has decreased.

Increasing the weight with changing the position of the anode from vertical to horizontal in the sample containing NPs indicates that the coating is denser. To compare and confirm the results for changing the weight and thickness, porosity was examined in different samples. Fig. 7 shows the porosity of the coated specimens in the horizontal and vertical positions of the anode. The reduction in porosity in the NPs is greater than in the MPs. This result is consistent with the previously obtained results. As mentioned in the previous results, increasing the weight and decreasing the thickness can be deduced from the increases in density. Also, using the additives in the presence of MPs has a small effect on porosity. The filling of pores has an enormous effect on the properties of the coating such as corrosion resistance which will be discussed in the following.

Due to the volcanic structure of PEO coatings, these coatings have a high roughness and their roughness is reduced by the addition of the NPs and MPs. When the amount of incorporation increases from its optimal level, the surface roughness does not change. The roughness average (Ra) of the coated samples in horizontally and vertically positions of the anode are shown in Fig. 8 . The Ra of the coated sample was equal to 0.1 µm in the horizontal position. It was the lowest roughness among the coated samples and led to the creation of a more uniform surface. The roughness of the sample with MPs was about 0.4 µm, which is approximately equal to the PEO coating created in the absence of particles. This roughness indicates the improper incorporation of these particles. Results show that the urea additive does not affect the properties of the coating, so the results of the samples in the absence of additives are presented in the following.

Furthermore, XRD analysis was performed to investigate the phases formed in the PEO film due to particle incorporation. Since the horizontal position in the presence of NPs is the best condition for the sample, only XRD analysis was taken from this sample. The XRD pattern for this sample was shown in Fig. 9 . According to the obtained results, MgO, Al2O3, and Mg3(PO4)2 peaks can be observed. The MgO peak is related to the oxide layer formed on the substrate during the process. In the PEO process, the substrate acts as the anode, under a strong electric field, magnesium is dissolved according to Eq. (1), and magnesium ions are formed [25,26].
(1)
Mg → M
g
2 +
+ 2
e
−

The penetration of magnesium ions within the electrolyte includes oxygen and phosphate ions is the reason for the formation of oxide film on magnesium. Eqs. (2) and (3) lead to the formation of magnesium oxide and magnesium phosphate, respectively.
(2)
M
g
2 +
+
O
2 −
→ MgO
(3)
3 M
g
2 +
+ 2
PO 4
3 −
→ M
g 3
( P
O 4
)
2
One of the peaks in the XRD pattern is related to the alumina NPs in the electrolyte. This peak can be formed in high concentration during the coating process.

Fig. 10 shows the EDS analysis of coating with NPs in mapping mode. According to the obtained results, it is clear that the main elements in coatings are Mg, O, P, and Al. These elements originate from compounds that be formed during PEO coating treatment. The source of magnesium is the substrate, whereas both oxygen and phosphorus are originated from the electrolyte. According to the elemental mapping, all elements are homogeneously distributed across the coating. The presence of Al indicates the incorporation of alumina NPs from the electrolyte into the coating. The filling of micro-pores has an enormous effect on the properties of the coating such as corrosion resistance. In the previous results, it was proven that adding alumina NPs to electrolytes resulted in the creation of alumina, phosphate, magnesia phases in the coating as evident in XRD results.

A diagram to provide the mechanism of NPs incorporation on the surface of the PEO coating based on the anode position is shown in Fig. 11 . The results showed that by changing their position from vertical to horizontal, NPs are better adsorbed, which is because, in the vertical position, the rotational motion of the solution (mechanical disturbance) causes the nanoparticles to move away from the sample interface. Electrolyte, while in the horizontal motion of the solution, helps to place more nanoparticles at the interface between the sample and the solution. As mentioned before, adding urea horizontally did not change the incorporation of nanoparticles, which could be due to the saturation of the coating with NPs and the lack of a preferred location. About MPs, due to the weight of these particles and the dimensions of the particles and the dimensions of the coating cavities, the probability of incorporation is low and even with changing the position of the sample, this incorporation does not increase.

PDP curves of the coated specimens under different conditions are shown in Fig. 12 . Current density, potential, as well as slopes of the anode and cathode regions are important parameters for the evaluation of corrosion resistance [45]. Tafel slopes obtained from the curves are shown in Table 4 (cathodic slope from -20 mV vs. OCP to -50 mV vs. OCP and anodic slope from +20 mV vs. OCP to +50 mV vs. OCP). The polarization resistance (Rp) is calculated by Stern's Gary's equation Eqs. (4) and ((5)) [46,47].
(4)
I
c o r r
=
β
R p
(5)
β =
β a
β c
2.3
(
β a
+
β c
)

Details of the pitting corrosion of magnesium in chlorine corrosive ions have been reported in various Refs. [48,49]. PEO coating acts as a barrier and protective layer on the surface against the penetration of corrosive ions. According to the mentioned issue, decreasing corrosion current density and increasing corrosion resistance will be occurred. Increasing the corrosion resistance depends on the oxide layer properties such as structure and density of the layer. By increasing the density of the layer, the penetration of corrosive ions into the substrate will be more difficult. So the corrosion resistance will be increased. Discharge channels or micro-voids are suitable routes for the penetration of corrosive ions toward the substrate in PEO coatings.

Due to obtaining results from the PDP measurement, it can be calculated horizontal-coated samples had higher corrosion resistance than vertical-coated samples. The corrosion potential of the coated samples with particles (NPs and MPs) was more positive. So, it stands as a reason for the lower tendency to corrosion. It was clear that corrosion current density has been reduced after the addition of alumina to the electrolyte. According to the obtained results from Fig. 3, adding the alumina NPs with different dimensions led to the filling of voids and obstruction of these routes. It's observed that the corrosion resistance of the samples was increased significantly after coating in the presence of alumina particles. Table 4 shows an increase in corrosion resistance numerically. The formation of the oxide film limited the incorporation of corrosive ions and reduced charge transfer in the metal-electrolyte interface. It can be established as the mechanism responsible for improving corrosion resistance. The corrosion resistance of coatings is affected by characteristics of the oxide film including chemical composition, thickness, defects structure, and chemical composition of the corrosive medium.

EIS method was applied for the accurate evaluation of the corrosion mechanism of the coated specimen with different particle sizes and positions as well as comparing with PDP test results. The Nyquist, bode, and bode-phase plots obtained from EIS measurements are shown in Figs. 13 and 14
, respectively. An equivalent electrical circuit (EEC) was utilized to fit the data depicted in Fig. 15 . The data extracted from the quantitative EEC have been represented in Table 5 . As can be seen in Fig. 15, a three-time constant circuit model has been suggested for all of the samples (except the N-H sample) which is acceptable due to the coating structure.

In our samples, the structure of PEO coating was be formed from PEO outer layer, PEO inner layer, and an interface between the oxide layer and the magnesium substrate or the electrical double layer (EDL). It can be noted that Ro, Ri, and Rct are related to the resistance of the PEO outer layer, PEO inner layer, and EDL, respectively. CPEo, CPEi, and CPEct are also related to the capacitive behavior of the PEO outer layer, PEO inner layer, and EDL. For the N-H sample (Fig. 15b), a two-time constant EEC model was used. Magnesium is corroded in the corrosive solution because of its low corrosion resistance and high reactivity. The pitting corrosion (resulted from the aggressive ion in the corrosion process) leading to the use of an inductor in the EEC [50]. The Rs value indicates the resistance of the solution in the proposed EEC which is specified in Table 5. Represented Chi-squared values or χ2 parameters indicate that the accuracy of the fitting process (Table 5). The low value for this parameter displays the high precision of fitting. The constant phase element has been used instead of a net capacitor due to the non-homogeneity of the surfaces and the exit from the ideal conditions [51,52]. Here, the impedance of the constant phase element is described as follows:
(6)
Z
C P E
=
1
T
(
j ω
)
n
Where T is the admittance constant, j represents an imaginary unit, ω is the angular frequency and n is the empirical power of CPE which ranges from 0 to 1. Zero or one shows pure resistance or capacitor, respectively.

As shown in Nyquist plots, semi-circular curves related to PEO layers are distinguishable in specimens. Semi-circles related to EDL represented the low radiuses, so it's not visible. Continued Nyquist curves at the downside of the Z' axis confirmed the presence of the inductor in the EEC. In pitting corrosion, magnesium is very sensitive. Therefore, an inductor (L) was applied to indicate localized corrosion at the interface of the substrate/coating. Also, the N-H coated sample represents the largest diameter in the Nyquist diagram. And after that, these belong to N-V, M-H, and M-V, respectively. The smallest diameter is related to the specimen coated without particles in the PEO process. Since the diameter of the Nyquist curve represents the Rp [53], the most corrosion resistance is attributed to the N-H specimen. After the N-H specimen, N-V, M-H, and M-V samples are respectively. As previously mentioned NPs complete filling of cracks and coating defects in the horizontal position. So, based on these observations, NPs have a good effect on corrosion resistance. Also, the horizontal sample containing NPs had the highest corrosion resistance. The obtained results for this section are in complete agreement with the obtained results from the polarization test.

The total resistance of the created coating can be compared with the other coatings at a frequency of 0.01 Hz. As shown in the bode curve (Fig. 14a), the total impedance of the coatings is related to the N-H sample. The numerical value of the total coating resistance is shown in Table 5.

The incorporation of NPs filled the pores of the coating and reduces the movement of the invading ions. So it leads to an increase in the total impedance of the coating and thus improves corrosion behavior. The obtained results from this test are in complete agreement with the results of morphological characterization.

The Bode-phase curve is illustrated in Fig. 14b. Three-time constants are detectable in the bode-phase curves. The first-time constant is almost observable in high frequencies for coated specimens. The second-time constant can be noticed in the lower frequency approximately from 10 to 100 Hz and the third one is visible in ever lower frequencies. First, second, and third-time constants for the specimens without particles are visible at higher frequencies. The results indicate that aggressive ions can easily and swiftly attack the coated layers and reach the substrate. Phase angle diagrams of the N-H specimen were reached at -90o. So these diagrams represented the capacitive behavior. According to the observation, these values were decreased in the other samples. The phase angle (as a criterion for examining the coating behavior) shows that the N-H sample is the most negative phase angle and the most capacitive behavior. Therefore, it is a better kind of corrosion behavior.

It can be concluded that PEO coatings have a dense inner layer and an outer porous layer. In the absence of more additive particles, the dense inner layer is responsible for corrosion resistance. Corrosion resistance increases significantly if the additive particles fill the porous of the outer layer and cover the defects of the coating. In the N-H sample, alumina particles were able to fill the cavities in the coating. Due to this fact, corrosive ions penetrate the coating or delay the movement of ions. So it can be concluded, corrosion resistance increases dramatically.

Fig. 16 shows the friction coefficient versus the distance for the coated samples under different conditions. It is observed that the PEO sample has an increasing behavior and high pulsation in the lower 100 m. Due to the high hardness of alumina particles, adding this additive into the coating increased the hardness and coefficient of friction of the coating.

According to the obtained results, MPs show better wear behavior compare to NPs. Due to their hardness and high dimensions, MPs are more involved with the pin and are removed easily from the coating during the test. According to the previous results, the coating created in the horizontal position has a higher friction coefficient than the vertical position, which indicates better incorporation of particles and improvement of the quality of the coating created in this position.

Fig. 17 shows the wear rate diagram of the sampled coated in different conditions. The minimum wear rate is related to the sample coated in the presence of NPs and in the horizontal position, which can be interpreted according to the previous results. The presence of NPs during the coating process caused all preferred locations for nucleation and crack growth to be blocked (Fig. 3), so the wear rate in these samples decreased significantly compared to other samples. The other parameters such as porosity, cracks, surface roughness, and thickness can affect the wear properties of the coating. In this study, with the addition of alumina (NPs and MPs) during the coating process, mechanical properties were improved in both horizontal and vertical positions. Yu et al. [53–56] explained that the presence of voids and preferred locations caused cracking, crushing, and collapsing of the coating during the wear test.

The results of wear tests are shown in Table 6 . Column "worn thickness" is related to the thickness of worn coating after the wear test. The thicknesses of the worn layers of different specimens are consistent with the results obtained for the specimens with lower wear rates than lose less thickness at lower distances. In Table 6, the lowest worn thickness is associated with the N-H.

SEM images of the specimens after the wear test are shown in Fig. 18 . As it can be observed, the presence of alumina nanoparticles in the coating has a positive effect on the wear behavior of the coating. Fig. 18 shows that the coating without alumina particles is fully worn after the wear test and even reaches the magnesium alloy substrate. Unlike the specimen without alumina particles, the initial failure is not observed in the coating containing alumina particles. Samples with alumina nanoparticles have an abrasive wear mechanism (samples N-H and N-V). In the case of samples with alumina microparticles, the dominant wear mechanism is adhesive (samples M-N and M-V). As it can be observed, the depth of this sinking is associated with the sinking depth measured by the roughness measurement device.

In this study, the effect of sample position on the amount of particle incorporation (in the presence of nano and micro alumina particles) was investigated during coating by the plasma electrolytic oxidation (PEO) method. The obtained results from this work were listed as the following:
•
The Maximum amount of particle incorporation in the coating was obtained in the horizontal position in the presence of nanoparticles (NPs).
•
There is no significant effect on the incorporation of NPs and micro-particles in the implement the additives (due to the filling of preferred locations).
•
The effect of additives on the incorporation of NPs can be investigated in the presence of preferred locations.
•
The incorporation rate of NPs is higher than microparticles (MPs) in the implied coating method.
•
Using the particles during the coating process leads to the improvement of corrosion resistance.
•
The utilization of particles was improved the abrasive properties such as reduction of wear rate during the coating process.
•
Using the NPs created better mechanical properties and corrosion behavior in the coating than MPs.
•
Coating in a horizontal position leads to the improvement of coating quality compared to the vertical position.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: No potential conflict of interest was reported by the author(s).
